ground_truth.title: The Year in Gardening: Looking Back With Joy (and Looking Ahead With Hope)
ground_truth.author: Melissa Ozawa
ground_truth.main_content: This is part of a series with Perfect Earth Project, a nonprofit dedicated to toxic-free, ecological gardening... Even for the most optimistic of us, it can be hard to stay positive these days. But hope, it is 'the thing with feathers,' and there are moments that can make us soar with joy, propelling us forward: the successful bans on pesticides like neonicotinoids; the sweet burst of a juneberry still warm from the sun; the return of the whooping crane after it teetered on the cusp of extinction. We asked a selection of our ecological gardening friends to share what keeps them going—plus one small thing we can all do in our yards next year to keep the momentum going. Jeff Lorenz and Kayla Fell of Refugia share: 'At this time of year, we are enamored by the stories laid bare in the landscape. Perennials that are left standing through winter are permeated with narrative: tales of shelter and sustenance, dynamic encounters of fullness and decay. While seemingly silent, they are instead performing amazing feats of survival and renewal out of sight—just like us!—silvery-white seed tufts, seedheads glittering with frost. Winter celebrates delicate details that we often miss during the showier exuberant summer months.' Uli Lorimer, Director of Horticulture at Native Plant Trust, reflects on the pink firework blossoms of Monarda fistulosa that reliably draw hummingbird clearwing moths to the garden, emphasizing how the garden provides an appropriate host plant for the larval stage and is managed without pesticides.
with: Even for the most optimistic of us, it can be hard to stay positive these days. But hope, it is 'the thing with feathers,' and there are moments that can make us soar with joy.
The successful bans on pesticides like neonicotinoids; the sweet burst of a juneberry still warm from the sun; the return of the whooping crane after it teetered on the cusp of extinction.
Perennials that are left standing through winter are permeated with narrative: tales of shelter and sustenance, dynamic encounters of fullness and decay.
The pink firework blossoms of Monarda fistulosa reliably draw hummingbird clearwing moths to the garden, and this image I find absolutely magical.
Connectivity for plants, wildlife, insects, and people is the most powerful tool we have.

items.alt: The fluffy seedheads of the grass Andropogon ternarius &#8\2\16;Black Mountain&#8\2\17; almost glow in the winter light along with the red branches of the red-twig dogwood (Cornus sericea) & &#8\2\16;Shenandoah&#8\2\17; switchgrass (Panicum virgatum) in the background and dense blazing star (Liatris spicata) in foreground. Photograph courtesy of Refugia.
items.caption: The fluffy seedheads of the grass Andropogon ternarius 'Black Mountain' almost glow in the winter light along with the red branches of the red-twig dogwood and 'Shenandoah' switchgrass in the background and dense blazing star in foreground. Photograph courtesy of Refugia.
Hummingbird clearwing moth visits a wild bergamot (Monarda fistulosa). This beautiful perennial also attracts birds, including hummingbirds, and butterflies. Photograph by Uli Lorimer.

items.alt: Above: The winter seed heads of purple coneflowers on the High Line. Photo by Timothy Schenk, courtesy of the High Line.
items.caption: The winter seed heads of purple coneflowers on the High Line.
